Hearing RE: Plaintiff WING INFLATABLES, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION's renewed motion for sanctions, forensic examination, and order to show cause re: contempt.

Appearances (via Zoom):

Michael T. Boardman, Esq., Baker & McKenzie LLP, (310) 201-4728, for Plaintiff.

Tad Devlin, Esq., KAUFMAN, DOLOWICH & VOLUCK, LLP, (415) 926-7600, for Defendant.

Ruling - ARGUED. At the conclusion of argument, Plaintiff WING INFLATABLES, INC.'s renewed motion for sanctions, forensic examination, and order to show cause re: contempt is denied with prejudice and a discovery referee is to be appointed, as articulated in the judge pro tem's report, which is adopted, is submitted. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order.

The court and the parties then discuss the pending Discovery motions. At the conclusion of discussion, the court advises that it intends to appoint a discovery referee and that all discovery is stayed until one is appointed. The court instructs counsel to meet and confer regarding agreeing to a discovery referee and that if counsel is unable to agree, each counsel is to submit the names of three distinct discovery referee's and the court will choose one to appoint. The court sets a hearing on the status of appointment of a discovery referee on Jul-20-2023 at 9:30 a.m. in Dept. 302.

Judge: Richard B, Ulmer Jr., Clerk: R. Woods, Court Reporter: Sheila Pham, CSR: 13293, T: (415) 274-9977, E: sheilapham28@gmail.com. (302/RBU)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar Aug-13-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Mark Goodman, Esq. (415.576.3000; Mark.Goodman@bakermckenzie.com) of Baker & McKenzie LLP for Plaintiff.
Laura Ruettgers, Esq. (415.926.7600; lruettgers@kdvlaw.com) of Kaufman Dolowich & Voluck, LLP for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT CERTAIN UNDERWRITERS AT LLOYD'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

Defendant CERTAIN UNDERWRITERS AT LLOYD'S, Subscribing to Insurance Policy No. TLH001130428 ("Lloyd's") motion to quash is denied. Plaintiff has the burden to demonstrate sufficient minimum contacts for jurisdiction. (See Coulston v. Cooper (1966) 245 Cal.App.2d 866, 868.)

Plaintiff (through the French declaration) demonstrates specific jurisdiction over Lloyd's. Lloyd's intentionally reached out to plaintiff, a California resident, to sell insurance to it regarding the purchase of another entity. The purpose of the insurance was to protect plaintiff against misrepresentations and overpaying for the entity. That was a California business risk. Plaintiff's harm (alleged overpayment) occurred in California, the insurance negotiations occurred here, and Lloyd's alleged bad faith also occurred here. Because (1) the Lloyd's defendants purposefully availed themselves of the privilege of doing business in California and (2) plaintiff's claims arise out of the Lloyd's defendants' contacts with California, the exercise of specific jurisdiction is warranted. (See Jayone Foods, Inc. v. Aekyung Industrial Co. Ltd (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 543, 554; McGee v. International Life Ins. Co. (1957) 355 U.S. 220 [exercise of jurisdiction over out-of-state insurer proper].) Lloyd's fails to show that the exercise of jurisdiction over it would be unreasonable even though the policy includes a Delaware choice of law provision and the parties agreed that this matter could also be litigated in Delaware. The composition of Lloyd's is also of no moment since all of its subscribing members were acting jointly to provide plaintiff the insurance.

Lloyd's alternative motion to stay/dismiss this action based on forum non conveniens is denied.

As a California resident, plaintiff's choice of forum is entitled to great deference. (See National Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 917 [California plaintiff's "choice of forum should rarely be disturbed unless the balance is strongly in favor of the defendant."]; Stangvik v. Shiley Inc. (1991) 54 Cal.3d 744, 754 [California "has a strong interest in assuring its own residents an adequate forum for the redress of grievances."].)

While Lloyds' demonstrates that Delaware is a suitable alternative forum, it fails to establish that the public and private factors weigh in its favor. Plaintiff's witnesses and documents are located in California and Lloyd's, an entity located in the United Kingdom, does not sufficiently show why litigating this bad faith matter in Delaware is more desirable.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-13-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Mark Goodman, Esq. (415.576.3000; Mark.Goodman@bakermckenzie.com) of Baker & McKenzie LLP for Plaintiff.
Laura Ruettgers, Esq. (415.926.7600; lruettgers@kdvlaw.com) of Kaufman Dolowich & Voluck, LLP for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant CFC UNDERWRITING LIMITED's ("CFC") demurrer to complaint is sustained with leave to amend as to all four causes of action for breach of contract, bad faith, violation of the UCL, and declaratory relief. (See CCP 430.10(e).)

Paragraph 7 of the complaint alleges that "Certain Underwriters at Lloyd's" ("Lloyd's") were the underwriters that issued the insurance policy to plaintiff. Paragraph 8 alleges that CFC acted as an underwriter and agent of Lloyd's. On demurrer, the court will generally defer to plaintiff's construction of an agreement where the instrument is ambiguous and reasonably susceptible to plaintiff's interpretation. (See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc. (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239.) Nevertheless, "[u]nder the doctrine of truthful pleading, the courts will not close their eyes to situations where a complaint contains allegations of fact inconsistent with attached documents, or allegations contrary to facts that are judicially noticed. False allegations of fact, inconsistent with annexed documentary exhibits [citation] or contrary to facts judicially noticed [citation], may be disregarded...." (Hoffman v. Smithwoods RV Park, LLC (2009) 179 Cal.App.4th 390, 400 (citations and internal quotations omitted).)

Here, the insurance policy attached to the complaint as exhibit A shows that CFC is not a party to the agreement. Exhibit A shows that the insurance was "effected through" CFC. The Declarations page does not list CFC as an underwriter of insurance; rather CFC is referred to as the "CLAIMS MANAGER[]." The policy's preamble states that the policy is between "you and us" and definition 41 of the policy defines "us" as "the Underwriters stated in the Declarations page." Under these circumstances, plaintiff cannot genuinely allege that CFC was a party to the agreement as an insurer. The only reasonable inference is that CFC was acting as Lloyd's agent and/or claims manager and therefore, cannot be individually liable for Lloyd's alleged transgressions. (See Gruenberg v. Aetna Insurance Company (1973) 9 Cal.3d 566, 576; Iversen v. Superior Court (1976) 57 Cal.App.3d 168, 171.)

Plaintiff argues in its opposition that it entered into an independent underwriting contract with CFC. The Court grants plaintiff 20 days leave to amend to plead claims based on that alleged agreement.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
